steps.text: Encase the sensing tip of the sensor in glass or PTFE, if needed.
Keep the sample transfer lines as short as possible.
Maintain the sample transfer lines at a temperature of 120 °C ± 14 °C (248 °F ± 25 °F) using active heating when necessary.
Orient the sample transfer lines with the downstream end lower than the upstream end so that any condensate will flow away from the filter and into the condenser.
Orient the condenser to cause moisture to flow down to the adsorbent module to facilitate condensate drainage.
Use a bath pump circulating system capable of providing chilled water flow to the condenser and adsorbent module water jackets.
Verify the function of the water circulating bath system.
Quantify unresolved isomers using the nearest eluting target PCB pre-extraction standard in Appendix A of this method, unless otherwise specified in applicable rule, regulation, or permit.
Resolve and report the 17 toxic PCB congeners, and report other PCB as totals by homolog (e.g., total TrCB).
Use a data system compatible with the mass spectrometer and capable of sequencing and monitoring multiple groups of selected ions.
Use an analytical balance to measure within 0.1 milligram (mg).
Rinse silica gel with methylene chloride.
Activate the silica gel by heating for at least 1 hour at 180 °C (356 °F).
Allow the silica gel to cool.
Rinse the silica gel sequentially with methanol and toluene.
Heat the rinsed silica gel at 50 °C (122 °F) for 10 minutes.
Increase the temperature gradually to 180 °C (356 °F) over 25 minutes.
Maintain the gel at 180 °C (356 °F) for 90 minutes.
Allow to cool in a desiccator to room temperature.
Store in a glass container with a PTFE-lined screw cap.
Combine 100 g of activated silica gel with 44 g of concentrated sulfuric acid in a clean screw-capped glass container.
Agitate thoroughly.
Disperse the solids with a stirring rod until obtaining a uniform mixture of acid-coated silica gel.
Store the mixture in a glass container with a PTFE-lined screw cap.
Combine 30 g of 1 N sodium hydroxide with 100 g of activated silica gel in a clean screw-capped glass container.
Agitate thoroughly.
Disperse solids with a stirring rod until obtaining a uniform mixture of base-coated silica gel.
Store the mixture in a glass container with a PTFE-lined screw cap.
Follow the procedures in ASTM D6911–15 for packaging and shipping environmental samples.
Follow the chain of custody procedures in ASTM D4840–99(2018)e1 for all samples.
Store sampling train rinses and filter samples in the dark at the storage conditions in Table 23–10 from the time the laboratory receives the samples until analysis.
Store adsorbent samples for PCDD/PCDF or PCB analysis prior to extraction in the dark at 6 °C (43 °F) or less for up to one year from the time the laboratory receives the samples.
Protect adsorbent samples destined for PAH analysis from ultraviolet light.
Store adsorbent samples for PAH analysis in the dark at 6 °C (43 °F) or less for up to 30 days from the time the laboratory receives the samples.
Analyze PAH extracts within 40 days of extraction.
Store sample aliquots, including archived extracts of PCDD/PCDF, PAH and/or PCB samples, in the dark at -10 °C (14 °F) or less for up to one year.

Perform an MDL determination using a minimum of seven spiked combined filter/sorbent media samples, spiked within 2 to 10 times of the expected MDL, and seven LMBs (combined filter/sorbent media) through all the steps of the method following the requirements in 40 CFR part 136 Appendix B.
Confirm target compounds meet the qualitative identification criteria in Sections 13.12 and 13.13.

Confirm newly determined MDLs by preparing a low-level spiked combined filter/sorbent media sample by spiking the sorbent with native target compounds at 1 to 5 times the MDL and pre-extraction standard at the concentration used to analyze field samples and analyze.
Prepare, extract, and analyze seven replicate spiked samples in a valid Extraction Batch.
Fortify the spiked samples near the midpoint of the initial calibration curve.
Demonstrate accuracy using the same set of replicate data generated for...

Calibrate the HRGC/HRMS system using a minimum of five concentrations over a range that brackets expected field sample concentrations and the concentration of isotopically labeled standards in spiked samples.
Determine the initial relative response factors for the target compounds and isotopically labeled standards using the initial calibration.
Monitor the QC check ions specified in Tables 23-4, 23-5, and 23-6 to verify instrument stability during the analysis.
Perform additional cleanup of the sample extract (or archive extract) and reanalysis.
Prepare the CCV standard at the same time as the batch of field samples using the same labeled standards.
Prepare CCV standards at mid-level of the calibration (C3 level from Tables 23–11, 23–12, or 23–13).
Spike the adsorbent with the appropriate pre-extraction standard to result in the final sample extract concentrations shown in Tables 23–7, 23–8, and 23–9 of this method, as applicable.
Transfer the adsorbent material to the glass extraction thimble in the glass solvent rinse catch beaker.
Rinse the module into the thimble in the beaker with the contents of Container No. 1.
Alternatively, suspend the adsorbent module directly over the extraction thimble in a beaker, then, using a wash bottle containing methanol, flush the XAD–2 into the thimble onto the filter.
Thoroughly rinse the interior of the glass module that contained the XAD–2 with toluene.
Identify specific PCB congeners.
Report all concentration calculations from the reanalyzed sample.
Verify that the RRT of each native to its labeled compound is within 0.006 RRT units compared to the corresponding RRTs in the continuing calibration.
Verify that the signals for the characteristic ion listed in Table 23–5 of this method are present.
Measure the response of each native target compound and the corresponding pre-extraction standard.
Calculate the mass of each target compound using the CCV RRF and equations in Section 12.7 of this method.
Use the pre-extraction standard to correct the native target compounds result for variations in performance of the extraction, cleanup, and concentration steps of the analysis.
Use Equation 23–2 to calculate the RRF for pre-extraction standard.
Calculate the Average RRF for Each Compound Over the Minimum of Five Calibration Levels.
Calculate the Percent RSD of the RRFs for a Compound Over the Calibration Levels.
Calculate the Standard Deviation of the RRFs for a Compound Over the Calibration Levels.
Calculate the Percent Difference of the RRF of the Continuing Calibration Verification Compared to the Average RRF from the Initial Calibration for Each Target Compound using Equation 23–1.
Calculate the Amount of Individual Target Compound i in the Sample by Isotope Dilution (pg).
Calculate the Concentration of the Individual Target Compound or Group i in the Emission Gas (pg/dscm).
Calculate the total concentration of a target compound group in the sample by substituting CT from Eq. 23–12 for Ci in Equation 23–8.
Use Equation 23–9 to determine the recovery of pre-sampling adsorbent standard and the pre-extraction filter recovery standard.
Use Equation 23–10 to determine the recovery of the pre-extraction standard.
Select the labeled standard having the same number of chlorine substituents and the closest retention time to the target analyte in question as the labeled standard to use for quantitation.
Remove both the salt solution and any residual extractable chemicals used in the polymerization process before use.
Prepare the resin by a series of water and organic extractions, followed by careful drying.
Perform the extraction using a Soxhlet extractor or other apparatus meeting Section 13.1 requirements, using an all-glass thimble containing an extra-coarse frit.
Retain the resin in the extractor cup with a glass wool plug and stainless-steel screen.
Place resin in a suitable container, soak for approximately 5 min with Type II water, remove fine floating resin particles and discard the water.
Fill with Type II water a second time, let stand overnight, remove fine floating resin particles, and discard the water.
Extract with water for 8 hr.

preconditions.text: The sensing tip protrudes at least 1.3 centimeters (cm) (1–2 in.) into the sample gas exiting the filter.
Alternative conditioning procedure may be used if the performance criteria in Section 13.1 are met for target compounds.
Failure to maintain the lock-mass during analysis.
If an interference with PFK m/z 223.9872 occurs, precluding meeting 10:1 S/N for the DiCB congeners at the optional Cal 1 level.
The retention time of the native congener must be within 0.006 relative retention time (RRT) units of the pre-extraction standard.
If you choose another column other than the SPB-Octyl column

constraints.text: The sample transfer line must be heat traced and constructed of glass or PTFE with connecting fittings that form leak-free, vacuum-tight connections without using sealing greases or tapes.
Mass drifts of 5 parts per million (ppm) or more can have serious effects on instrument performance.
Pay special attention to cleanliness during transport, field handling, sampling, recovery, laboratory analysis, and preparation of the adsorbent cartridges to avoid contamination.
Sample extracts must not be stored with pierced septa.
Demonstration is repeated for failed compounds only.

The relative standard deviation (RSD) for the mean calibration relative response factor from each of the unlabeled analytes and isotopically labeled compounds used in an analysis must be less than or equal to the values in Table 23–14.

The signal-to-noise (S/N) ratio for the GC/MS signal present in every selected ion current profile must be greater than or equal to 10 in all concentrations of calibration standards for unlabeled targets and isotopically labeled standards.
The ion abundance ratios must be within the control limits in Table 23–15.
Spike into the adsorbent, not on top of the adsorbent.
Recovery of pre-extraction standard must meet the minimum specifications in Section 9.2 of this method.
The units for Qpe and Qn in Eq. 23–1 and the units for Qpa and Qpe in Equation 23–2 must be the same.
For the quantitation of the pre-sampling adsorbent standard and the pre-extraction filter recovery standard, use a corresponding pre-extraction isomer (or homolog) with the closest retention time.
The recovery performance criteria for these standards are in Sections 13.14, 13.15, and 13.16 of this method.
Recovery may be calculated based on mass instead of concentration, as needed.

Rpe must be corrected for the fraction of the original sample extract used for analysis (e.g., if half of the extract is used for analysis of the target class, Rpe must be multiplied by a factor of 2).

Unless otherwise specified in applicable regulations, permits or other requirements, count any target compounds reported as non-detected as EDL when calculating the concentration of target compounds in the sample.
The frit is recessed 10–15 mm above a crenellated ring at the bottom of the thimble to facilitate drainage.
Because the resin floats on methylene chloride, carefully retain the resin in the extractor cup with a glass wool plug and stainless-steel screen.
